Luck Bomberger was working two part-time jobs in a grocery store and a bank. Don Reist his friend invited him to go to Europe on a cattle boat.

He said yes right then he knew it was an opportunity of a lifetime.

UNRRA hired "Seagoing Cowboys" to take care of the horses, young cows, and mules that were shipped overseas.

Luke signed up with his friend Don.

Before arriving in Greece, Luke turned 18 years old, which meant he could be drafted for military service.

When his draft board learned he was on a cattle-boat trip, they told him to keep doing that for his service.

By the time he was discharged in 1947, he had made 9 trips the most of any Seagoing Cowboy.

The cattle boat trips were an unbelievable opportunity for Luke.

Besides helping people, he had the side benefit of seeing Europe and China.

But seeing the Acropolis in Greece was special.

Luke toured an excavated castle in Crete and marveled at the Panama Canal on his way to China.

It took about two weeks to cross the Atlantic Ocean from the eastern coast of the United States and a month to get to China.

Caring for the animals during the crossings kept him busy.

They had to be fed and watered two or three times a day.

Bales of hay and bags of oats had to be pulled up from the lower holds of the ship.

Stalls had to be cleaned.

Helping out on his aunt Katie's farm as a boy had prepared him for hard work, but not for dangers at sea.

He couldn't work for a couple of days because of cracked ribs.

Being a Seagoing Cowboy was much more than an adventure for Luke.

It opened the world up to him.

He is grateful for the opportunity.

It made him more aware of people of other countries and their needs.

The awarness stayed with him, leading his family to host a number of international students and exchange visitors for many years.

essay_text:
Most people have, at some point, seen the Mona Lisa by Leonardo Da Vinci, not necessarily the real one in Paris, but some sort of duplicate. The piece is incredibly iconic with almost anyone in the digital era being able to recognize it at first glance. While Mona Lisa herself may be known for her smile, it could be possible that she's been faking it the entire time. In Nick D'Alto's article about recent facial recognition technology, he discusses the anatomical composition of the Mona Lisa's face, and it is indeed happy. With technology like this, real world situations could become much more convinient due to the ability for technology to immediately recognize our positive or negative reactions to certain things, in particular, student's reactions within classroom settings. Facial recognition technology can provide a much more engaging learning experience for students and is valuable to student's learning experiences as well as teacher's teaching abilities.

With the use of, as it's called, Facial Action Coding Scanner, computers can immediately identify how students feel about particular classroom aspects and activities. This creates an opportunity for teachers to edit and maximize the learning experience for students. It would also allow teachers to prescribe certain assignments to students who feel especially disconnected by providing extra assistance, or especially excited by getting them more involved with the material beyond that which is tought in a textbook. As Dr Huang says in the article: "A classroom computer could recognize when a student is becoming confused or bored." Based off of how the collective students feel about a particular topic or activity, the teacher could manipulate how they approach that topic or abort the activity if it is far too ineffective for classroom use. If students feel disengaged and become less interested in a topic as a result of the activity, it is vital that it be abandoned. This will allow students to stay engaged by providing them with assignments that pique their interest rather than damage their passion for a topic.

Beyond elementary use, FACS can develop a realistic model of the human face with almost any emotion using all 44 muscles in the face. This type of technology could be crucial for multiple types of courses across student's school careers. People in classes discussing sociology could identify the relationship between speech and facial features, such as how positive comments trigger muscles that imply happiness or lack thereof in the case of an impairment. Another critical use could be for children with underdeveloped social skills who are unable to immediately identify other's emotions like most children can. This is an extreme disability since, as Dr Huang says, "Most human communication is nonverbal, including emotional communication, so computers need to understand that, too." Children could learn to identify how the use of certain muscle's in other's faces symbolize certain emotions and other nonverbal communications such as pain or pleasure.

A valid response to the article could be to identify when the author talks about empathy and claims, "(Empathy) may happen because we unconsciously imitate another person's facial expressions." This evidence supports the idea that teachers themselves can identify the reactions of students to certain classroom settings and makes the computer-generated facial recognition software completely superfluous. Despite this, however, students can feel complex emotions, especially during transitional years, where emotions can be more difficult to recognize, even by psychology teachers. We can see this when the author describes the Mona Lisa's emotions at the beginning and says, "She's 83 percent happy, 9 percent disgusted, 6 percent fearful, and 2 percent angry." It is not readily apparent to one looking at the Mona Lisa that she is fearful, and, in the same way, it is not readily apparent to a teacher or counselor

if a student is completely uninterested. For situations like these, computers are able to identify anatomically when students feel certain emotions, rather than relying on the often inaccurate instincts of teachers.

A good teacher can get his students to memorize something, then repeat it on a test, but it takes far more effort to get one's students legitimately excited about topics even when the material has very little interest value. In order to maximize each students interest in a classroom setting, it's vital to understand how one's students feel about what one says or does. Since this cannot and should not be solely assessed by teachers, computer facial recognition is the path to success. By being able to help socially impaired children and engage students in concepts, the case for using FACS in the classroom is strong, not just for students but for our nation as a whole. Many refer to the youth of today as the future of America, and in order to have a bright future, schools ought to brighten our youth with passion. A bright student does not simply memorize information, they cherish it, they keep it, and they use it for their country to drive itself towards a better tomorrow.

essay_text:
Humans have been curious about what is out beyond the sky for a long time. From ancient Greek and Roman astronomers who thought the Earth was the center of the universe, to Galielio who proved them wrong, humans have been wondering about what is truly out there amoung the heavens and the stars. In the "The Challenge of Exploring Venus", the author, despite the many harrowing "challenges" metioned, strongly and effectively supports the idea that studying Venus is a worthy pursuit, calling to that innate human want to know.

One way he supports this endavor is through scientific logic. He cites how similar Venus is to earth saying, "Venus is the closest planet to Earth in terms of density and size, and occasionally the closest in distance too". One of the best ways to support a point is through factual evidence. By making clear that Venusian exploration makes logical sense, the author strengthens his argument. The fact that Venus is Earth-like and at times very close distance wise to her, makes exploring Venus seem like the next logical step. This scientific backing gives the mission to know more about Venus crediblity and adds a sense of noblitiy, which makes it seem worthy. Without scientific backing it would seem like a far-fetched dream, unworthy of any time or effort.

The author also effectively supports the exploration of Venus through emotional means. He does this by dangling the possibility of life on Venus. He states,"Venus was probably covered largely with oceans and could have supported various forms of life". It is basic human nature to want to not be alone. The want on community drives humans to great lengths to find others like them. Humans have and contiune to cross oceans, battle fields, and other challenging situations in order to not be by themselves. This pursuit for companionship extends to the exploration of Venus. Despite the temperature of Venus's surface being "over 800 degrees Fahernheit" and it having an "atmospheric pressure ... 90 times greater than [Earth's]" humans are still trying to think of ways to go there on the slim chance that there might be or might have been life there at one point. All in the hopes that humans might not be so alone in this grand universe. By tapping into the basic need and want to not be alone the author makes a strong case for the worthiness of Venusian exploration because many see the search for other life as noble cause and can relate with it.

Another way the author supports his point is by giving expamples of the progress already made and supplying hope. One of the "challenges" Venusian exploration faces is the weather on Venus. Venus has "eurpting vlocanoes, powerful earthquakes, and frequent lightning strkies". These condtions would frighten anyone. But to combat them, the author informs the reader about a possible solution proposed by NASA of a "blimp-like vehicle hovering 30 or so miles above the roiling Venusian landscape". In doing so, the author makes the problems seem easier to solve while at the same time providing hope through a possible solution. By showing that the exploration of Venus might indeed be possible that author is able reenforce the worthiness of pursuing Venus. The question changes from "Can it be done?" to "How should it be done?" which makes it seem like a worthy adventure. The author also give the pursuit hope by diplaying a possible way for it to actually happan. This can get people excited and add to the fervor and want to find out what Venus is really like.

Through the use of scientific logic, emotional motivation, and inspiring hope, the author of "The Challenge of Exploring Venus" is able to take an endavor that seems impossible and turn it into a worthy adventure that many can relate with and want to happen. The author taps into human curiosity and exploits it. In the same way the drive to explore the Moon enabled humans to reach new heights, so to does the author depict the study of Venus an epic mission, worthy of adverse challenges humans will face to accomplish it.

Why Driverless Cars Should be Developed

Science fiction has a tendency to predict many new technologies of the future, such as the cell phone, straight out of "Star Trek". One product feasable product that still is not on the market is the driverless car. Many car manufacturers are hard at work at developing these cars, but would they bring more harm than help? Driverless cars should be developed because they many possibilities of human error, they are safe, and because they allow the driver more leisure.

Human error is the main cause of auto-accidents, and the introduction of driverless cars would help to eliminate a lot of that problem. Imagine a scenario of a drunk driver. They wreck because they are intoxicated and it hinders their ablilty to drive. If the car had been driverless, then the car could have safely transported the intoxicated driver home. If the driverless cars were implemented, they would get rid of whole fields of auto-accidents. Drunk driving would be a thing of the past, the elderly could drive just as well as a younger driver if both had driverless cars. Injured persons could drive themselves around without aid. The article, "Driverless Cars Are Coming" helps to point this out when it says that Google's cars have "driven more than half a million miles without a crash..." (Driverless). This shows that the Google semi-driverless cars have almost eliminated auto-accidents, and they are not fully driverless. One could infer that if a fully driverless car were introduced, the cars would almost never crash, and most definately they would not crash as a fault of the driver. Altogether, if driverless cars were introduced, they would not allow a driver the chance to make a mistake that could harm themself or others because the car would drive for them.

With that in mind, safety of the driverless cars without the driver would now remain on the technology, and driverless-car technology is proven to be a reliable source of transportation. This can be seen from the same remark made on Google cars, where it says that they "have driven more that half a million miles without a crash" (Driverless). This shows that Google has extensively looked over the technology and that even with technology implemented in 2009 it can function close to flawlessly. If a driverless car were to be manufactured today with today's technology, it would be much better than Google's near-flawless 2009 system. So, driverless cars are safe because they have already proven themselves for being crash safe for more than half a million miles, and if one were to be manufactured today, it would be even more safe.

Along with that, the lack of a need for a driver would allow the driver to have spare time to himself, which would allow automakers to enhance the leisure of the car, as that would become the main factor in buying a car if the market was completely full of driverless cars. Consider that today the main selling point of a car is how well it can drive. Consider also that a driver today spends all of his time driving. If a driver in a driverless car market would spend most of his time entertaining him or her self, the selling point would be the cars ablilty to entertain the driver along with the rest of the passengers. With this the market would see the rapid improvement of in-car entertainment, much like how the car was enhanced enormously in its ability to drive from its creation to today. In conclution, a driverless car would allow a driver to spend more time in leisure, making leisure the main selling point of a car, which would in turn make the car companies work harder towards improving entertainment.

Hence, driverless cars would be better humans because they eliiminate the chance for human error, they are already proven to be safe on the roads and almost completely accident-free, and because they would allow the driver to spend more time with entertainment, allowing a rapid advance in that aspect of the car. Driverless cars are definately a large step up from the regular car and their developement should be considered largely, since driving is such a big portion of societies life.

From todays standpoint, driverless cars have yet to reach their market on a widescale platform. Companies such as Google, which are based around innovation and improvement on what the world has already seen are the primary targets in the progress of coming forward with a successful self-driving car. The use of other modern day technologies such as radar and binary coding have helped come nearer to a permanent solution of the crisis involving mass amounts of automobile accidents. The developement of driverless cars by Google and other companies is a positive production, as it will bring a solution to the issue of car accidents and create an easier, and possibly more affordable form of transportation for the consumers.

At the current pace in which technology is being innovated and produced, new concepts and designs are being taken off the production lines at a rate never before seen, and every day something fresh and new is being invented in all forms of every day life. From architectural design, to social media and devices to forms of transportation, change is happening, and most is for the better, and often times the safety of the user is the primary reason of these changes. Ensuring the safety of the consumer is a key necessity in creating a successful product, especially in the automotive industry, as crash ratings are typically the thing that will make or break a purchase. With self-driving cars, the issue of crashes could potentially become obsolete once most drivers make the transition. Many current car companies have begun using many concepts similar to those of self-driving cars, introducing sensors similar to those of Google's modified Toyota Prius to increase consumer safety. In this technology, if a driving car is nearing too close to another car, with no sign of slowing down, the sensor will detect the car as it closes in to the other car, and slow it down to a stop to prevent a crash. The world has already started the shift into the technology of driverless cars in this sense, as that action was not conceived by the driver, but the car itself. If the technologies of a self-driven car are fully implemented, the safety would not only increase, but create an easier form of transportation if all issues are fully refined and solved.

Many issues regarding cars are often caused by the user. For instance the lack of upkeep in the car, forgetting to buy gas, and reckless driving as well as bad use of signals and attention to signs are among the largest issues in the automotive world. With self driving cars, however, the job will have much less work to be done. Instead of having to pay attention to road signs and traffick lights, sensors could be inputted and outputted using simple binary, which is similar to an on and off switch, though can be used in a much more complex manner using radar. The radar could be coded to send an output for only certain cars to move at once, depending on their position away from the sensor, as Google explained, and the car could automatically input the route a driver wishes to proceed in the GPS system, and the radar could send signals using simple binary to indicate which direction each driver wishes to go, for example 1 could be straight, 10 could be left and 11 could be right. With the recent increase in production of roundabouts, the job could be made even easier, by absolving any type of road sign which was once hindering a car from continuing. All issues with transportation could be easily solved using newer technology with driverless cars, as most times, accidents are caused by man.

Overall, driverless cars are a positive idea, however they still need to be given much work before they are road ready. In the near future, driverless cars could be implemented all over the world, even in cities as busy as Dubai, the most well known city for traffick in the world. The solution could be an inexpensive and easy fix to a death crisis caused by reckless driving, and inspire a fix in technology among other regions of modern life.

essay_text:
Venus is an unknown planet, despite being so close to us. It is hostile, and no human could survive on its surface. But some think it is possible to reach the surface and study Venus up close. It has many dangers in the way of progress. It's clouds contain sulfuric acid, and it's thick atmosphere is made up of almost 97 percent carbon dioxide. The pressure on the surface in 90 times greater than that of Earth, and temperatures reach over 800 degrees Fahrenheit. It boasts the hottest surface tempuature of any planet in the solar system, and has erupting volcanos, powerful earthquakes, and frequent bursts of lightning that impeade probes and rovers exploring the surface. But some think it is still a possiblity to go to the hostile planet. The author of the article believes it is possible to reach and explore the surface despite the danger. The author supports the idea that studying Venus is a worthy pursuit despite the dangers in presents by explaining that it may have once been earth-like a very long time ago, that it is the closest planet to Earth for exploration, and how scientists are now testing ways to get on the surface without any casualties.

The author gives support by explaining the fact the surface of Venus was once earth-like, and that it had many of the features that Earth has on it's current surface. It is amazing to think that such a unsuitable planet could have once been eath-like, but being near as it is to Earth, it is not entirely impossible. The author tells how many have made comparisons to Earth based on it's current surface, "Astronomers are fascinated by Venus because of it may well once have been the most Earth-like planet in our solar system....Today Venus still has some features that are analogous to those on Earth. The planet has a surface of rocky sediment and includes familiar features such as valleys, mountains, and craters." The feature the author talks about are very Earth-like, so the possiblity of it once being like a "utopain" planet are not dim. He point out the valleys, mountains, and craters that can be seen around the surface. Maybe the craters were the cause of the hostility on the planet, and it's inhabitent were wiped off the planet by a imbargement of asteriods and possibly comets. And the crashs caused climate change, thus shifting the planet into it's demise for supporting life. But if it is so close, couldn't this be true, that Venus was once in the Goldilocks Zone, and while Earth formed, Venus was the planet of life. After all, it is our neighbor.

The author continues to support his ideas of exploration by pointing out the fact that Venus is, at most times, the closest planet to us, Mars being a bit more distant. It is often called our sister planet, as we have very much in common with Venus. The author explains how closly were related to Venus, and why we chose to explore it, "Often referred to as Earth's 'twin', Venus is the closest planet to Earth in terms of desity and size.....Futhermore, recall the Venus can sometimes be our nearest option for a planetary visit, a crucial consideration given the long time frames of space travel." It is about the same size, and relativly close, so it is one of the obvious choices. It is also noted that in space, time matters. If you don't get to the planet quick, your ship could lose oxygen, your could run out of food, or you might miss the planet when it goes through rotation, making harder to get home and less likely to reach the planet another time. Although it is hard to make the surface of Venus, it is our closest option and is more like us, thus making it more suitable. The question for scientists is how. This question may not be solved for a long time, but already, scientists are making advaces toward the next landing on Venus.

The author also supports his idea of Venus exploration by showing ways that people are making advaces toward a possible landing. New technologies and old technologies alike are being combined to make the landing last, and for futher exploration. The author shares some of the developments made by scientist recently, "Imagine a blimp like vehicle hovering 30 or so miles above the roiling Venusian landscape. Just as our jet airplanes travel at a higher altitide to fly over many storms, a vehicle hovering over venus would avoid the unfriendly ground condition by staying up and out of thier way." Another advance includes using new material the protect electronics and other computer equipment in the hostile environment, "NASA is working on other approaches to studying Venus. For example, some simplified electronics made of silicon carbide have been tested in a chamber simulating the choas of the Venus surface and have lasted for three weeks in such conditions." NASA has made significant advance in the pursuit of getting onto the most Earth-like hostile planet in the solar system. New discoveries continue to be made, and soon the expedition for Venus will be underway.

The author has supported his claim throughout the article that studying Venus would be a worthy pursuit despite the dangers it presents by explaining it's likeness to Earth, it's closeness in proximity, and how scientists are advancing closer toward the goal of landing. Humans have done things like this before. The 1969 moon landings, for example, were thought to be impossible, but through human drive and motivation, we achieved this goal, and now we are faced with a new challenge, Venus. But with the same human drive and motivation that put man on the moon, people will explore our "twin" planet, and uncover it's greatest secrets.

The Facial Action Coding System is one of the new technologies of this century that is very complex and futuristic. It uses algorithims and muscle knowledge to discern the emotion of people in reality, photos, and other facial sources as well. The system can calculate how much you're happy, sad, suprised, and angry. Knowing other people's emotions is a great tool and can be used in many different situations. This new technology is good for a lot of things, and being used in a classroom is one of them.

Students need to be focused and engaged at all times in the classroom because if they are neither engaged, nor focused then they will struggle with learning new things and excelling in and out of the classroom. A computer in a class constantly scanning students' faces could become a distraction, but it would solve more problems than it could create. These days students are more vulnerable to distractions, and there are a lot of uncontrollable factors they are standing against. Some do not get enough sleep, some do not get enough to eat, and some do not have good health, whether that be mentally, socially, emotionally, or physically. These factors can take a toll on their ability to focus and be receptive to new information being taught in the classroom. They easily can become "confused or bored"(D'alto 6) or even distracted in a class, but if there was a way to track the behaviors that coincide with those emotions then it would be easier to assist the students. With the computer scanning their faces students emotions would be much more clear, and, as D'alto said in paragraph 6, the needed adjustments could be made to help students pay attention so they can learn.

Many teachers find it hard to keep their lesson plans exciting and engaging because of their lack of resources, but with this computer it would be easy to recieve real feedback from students emotions on what they like and dislike. Moreover, even though the computer might present itself as a distraction in the beginning over time it would become a useful tool for the classroom, and be a huge asset for both students and teachers.

essay_text:
The Facial Action Coding System enables computers to read emotions, this is valuable to teachers in a classroom. Prof. Thomas Huang of the Beckman Institute for Advanced Science at the University of Illinois, and Prof. Nicu Sebe of the University of Amsterdam have invented a way for computers to read peoples emotions. This will be useful in the classroom because tehre are tons of students who hide how they really feel. Whether this be a negative way or not. This particular technology could potentially help counselors with students who are being bullied, or have problems at home.

The computer identifies 44 major muscles in the human face. Then compares the changes in these muscles to a neutral face. It said in the text that, "Eckman has classifies six basic emotions-- happiness, surprise, anger, disgust, fear, and saddness--" though these arent all the human emotions they definitly give a good idea as to what someone is feeling.

These scientists actually tested the machine on a famous painting by Da Vinci. This peculiar painting has been the topic of speculation for a long time. No one has been able to tell if she is happy, sad, or neutral. The painting is called the Mona Lisa. They said in the begining of the article that, "She's 83 percent happy, 9 percent disgusted, 6 percent fearful, and 2 percent angry."

This technology will be useful in classrooms because as technology advances it needs to be a little more human. What i mean by this is a computer may be giving a lesson, but it needs to know if students are confused, angry, or bored. This will give the computer reasons for explaining more, taking a break, or giving a fun game to bring the student's attention back. In the text is said, "Most human communication is nonverbal, including emotional communication."

They should introduce this emotion reading technology into classrooms. They should actually integrate it into the whole school system. Students and teachers are constantly on computers, maybe the computers should understand more than what is being typed in. This could potentialy help students with learning, anxiety, depression, and anger. Especially because every teen thinks that no one knows how they feel; at least their computer will.

essay_text:
The Facial Action Coding System is a great tool in decoding one's true emotions. The FACS could be used to detect boredom, confusion, interest, or happiness in students. Being able to calculate these emotions would be valuable to students and teachers in a classroom.

Not all students are interested in school and in fact most would rather be elsewhere than in a classroom. With the FACS teachers would be able to detect when students are bored and may be able to change the way they teach their lesson in order to make it less banal. Creating a more interesting lesson would in turn create more interested students, increasing happiness and decreasing boredom. Students may start to want to learn which would also improve grades or behavior in a classroom,

Not all students are equal when it comes to learning. Some young scholars may be a natural when it comes to learning while others struggle to understand material being taught. The FACS could detect when a student is confused and alert a teacher so that they may better explain the subject being taught. This feedback would almost prevent students from lacking in schoolwork and could help with student's grades.

Students who enjoy the classroom are more likely to be interested and succeed. A teacher could use the FACS to see when students like and dislike what or how they are being taught. Then the teacher could adjust accordingly in order to produce better results. If the teacher is able to get every student engaged in learning, it would boost happiness, behavior, and maybe even learning in the classroom.

The Facial Action Coding System's ability to read emotions would be a valuable asset to classrooms. Being able to detect confusion and boredom would be beneficial to both teachers and students. Making better lesson plans and improving the happiness of students would make going to school seem exciting and not bland. With the FACS being able to read different muscles and emotions the possibilities of improving the classroom are endless.

essay_text:
In "The Challenge of Exploring Venus," the author suggests that studying Venus is a worthy pursuit despite the dangers it presents. Giving examples of how Earth and Venus are quite similar and how exploring it closer will provide us better understanding into the planets near us. Stating how no spacecraft was able to survive landing on Venus. Yet, the author presents the idea of studying Venus is important despite not being able to explore it in the past. In the article, the author strongly supports his idea by providing two plans on how NASA could possibly explore Venus while also stating how to overcome the cons and dangers of exploring our neighboring planet.

Readers are introduced to the idea of how NASA has found a possible solution to study Venus while staying away from the harsh conditions on its surface. Stating in Paragrah five, "At thirty-plus miles above the surface, temperatures would still be toasty at around 170 degrees Fahrenheit, but the air pressure would be close to that of sea level on Earth. Solar power would be plentiful, and radiation would not exceed Earth levels. Not easy conditions, but survivable for humans." The author provides support to the main idea. The plan is stated to be survivable to humans and also allows NASA to see Venus in safe conditions. Overcoming the dangers Venus presents to explorers.

However, the author gives cons to researching Venus from a farther distance. Stating in Paragraph six, "...peering at Venus from a ship orbiting or hovering safely far above the planet can provide only limited insight on ground conditions because most forms of light cannot penetrate the dense atmosphere, rendering standard forms of photography and videography ineffective. More importantly, researchers cannot take samples of rock, gas, or anything else, from a distance." Therefore, providing many cons to the authors previous plan. While studying from afar does give researchers information about Venus, it does not give them detailed research. The plan does support the authors claims and goes above overcoming dangers, it just does not provide worthiness of exploring Venus.

Although, the author presents the idea of working out the cons of the last plan and providing the solutions to the cons in a new plan. Stating at the end of Paragraph six, "Many researchers are working on innovations that would allow our machines to last long enough to contribute meaningfully to our knowledge of Venus." Overcoming the cons and dangers in this previous plan, which provides strong support in the authors suggestion of studying Venus. In Paragraph seven, readers are presented with the different projects to find the perfect medium to use in creating a shuttle to land on Venus for further exploration. Stating, "For example, some simplified electronics made of silicon carbide have been tested in a chamber simulating the chaos of Venus's surface and have lasted for three weeks in such conditions. Another project is looking back to an old technology called mechanical computers." Once again, providing strong support towards the author's main idea.

The author presents a plan in the beginning of the article on how to explore Venus from afar, provides the cons to the orginal idea, then puts forth an idea to overcome both the dangers and cons of exploring Earth's "twin." Greatly supporting the authors suggestions that studying Venus is a worthy pursuit. There are no cons or dangers once the proper substance is found to be safe on the surface of Venus. Allowing the idea of a worthy, meaningful, and safe pursuit to Venus despite the dangers it presents.

essay_text:
The planet of Venus is considered to be a danger to humans given the conditions it is in. In the article " The Challenge of Exploring Venus", the author suggests that depsite the dangers presented by Venus, it would be a worthy pursuit to study the planet. The author helpfully establishes multiple key pieces of evidence supporting this claim such as Venus may have once been the most Earth-like planet in our solar system, Venus is the nearest option for a planetary visit, and trying to get to Venus is considered a challenge that could help humans prepare for even more extremely challenging environments.

In paragraph two, the author states "Often refered to as Earth's "twin," Venus is the closest planet to Earth in terms of density and size, and occasionally the closest distance too." This quote, along with this quote, "Long ago, Venus was probably covered largely with oceans and could have supported various forms of life, just like Earth." showcase exactly how alike Venus may have been to Earth. This could be helpful in studying the possibilities of what may eventually happen to planet Earth.

Not many planets are as close to Earth as Venus is and that is a crucial point when the amount of time space travel can take is taken into consideration. Humans have accomplished and gained much knowledge about space travel since the race to space began. Scientists' have meticulously studied everything they could learn about space and can even estimate how much time it would take to travel to different places in space. This is crucial when you take into consideration how much time, effort, and money goes into space travel. With Venus being so close to the Earth, and all those factors taken into consideration, it seems to be an obvious and intelligent choice to make that humans should not give up on travelling and studying Venus. More than anything, it would be a wasted opportunity.

While the idea of being able to study Venus seems daunting, perhaps it is only the lack of courage that is holding NASA back from doing it. Curiosity is a natural thing that all humans are born with, and it never goes away. This is relevant because with curiosity, the gain of knowledge can go hand in hand with it. Without curiosity, not even half of the things humans have done and developed would be possible. Shying away from Venus is doing more harm than good, and it will only serve to produce hesitancy at the sight of any challege.

The author of the Article "The Challenge of Exploring Venus," brings up many key points of evidence as to why Venus should be explored and studied, despite the dangers of it. Among those are, it is the closest planet to Earth relatively speaking, Venus is the closest distance, and the study of the planet Venus brings up a unique challenge to the human race that backing down from would be detrimental. All of the key points supports the author's idea that studying venus is a worthy pursuit despite the dangers.

essay_text:
The automobile is one of mankinds greatest innovations. Since its invention in the 20th century, it has made tasks such as commuting to work or visiting your friends simpler than ever. Just hop in your car and your gas-powered vehicle will have you there within minutes. Although they are handy, could too much of a good thing be a bad thing? In this case, I believe that this is true. Limiting car usage could have benefits such as easier and cheaper transportation and fewer greenhouse gas emissions.

Imagine a world where public transportation (subways, trains, buses) could be accessed easier. Now look past the ways of modern life and cars we've become so dependent on. The growth of public transportation in places other than large cities could have huge benefits to the communities. Cars would not be relied upon and not as much money would have to be spent on gas. Of course people would still use cars to get from point A to point B but they would see public transportation as more of a go-to option. (Source 4) "Bill Ford proposed a plan of partering with telecommunications industries to create cities in which pedestrian, bicycles, private cars, commercial and public transportation traffic are woven into a connected network to save time conserve resources, lower emissions and improve safety." I believe limiting car usage would therefore raise public transportation usage and allow us to commute safer and cheaper than before.

Pollution has become an increasingly important problem in recent years. With all the craze over whether "global warming" is imminent or not, many have been trying to find ways to cut down on greenhouse gas emissions. The United States holds claim to the invention of the automobile and also stands as one of the most car reliant countries averaging at least one per household. According to the text, passenger cars are responsible for 50 percent of greenhouse gas emissions in some car-intensive areas in the United States (Source 1). Reducing that number by ways of carpooling, public transport, and hybrid cars, would benefit our society and help to protect the world we live in for future generations. For example, (Source 2)after days of intensifying smog, Paris enforced a partial driving ban to clear the air of the global city one Monday. Motorists with even-numbered plates were told to leave their cars at home or face a $31 fine. In just one day, car traffic was down 60 percent with less people driving and the smog cleared enough to lift the ban for odd-numbered plates the next day. In Bogota, Cali, and Valledupar, Colombia a similar experiment was put to test where there was a full Day Without cars. This plan promoted alternative transportation (bicycles, walking, taxis, buses) and helped to reduce smog. A local businessman from Bogota saw the day as beneficial and states that "It's a good opportunity to take away stress and lower air pollution." (Source 3)

Although modern lifestyles have become overly dependent on cars, usage can be reduced and have many benefits to our society. While this would be a process that would no doubt take years to accomplish globally, I believe that it is very possible and likely that limiting car usage would lead to easier and cheaper transportation, and fewer greenhouse gas emissions or pollution.

essay_text:
Technology is constatnly advancing and evolving. Advanced technology allows peolpe to accomplish tasks faster, with little effort. Although technology makes life easier, it takes away from the human aspect of things. Driverless cars are one example of advanced technology that limits human interaction with technology. This technology is a great advance in automechanics, but overall, it is unwise and dangerous to impliment driverless cars into society.

Traditionally, driving is an activity that requires thought and reasoning. Some situations encountered while driving are more than a simple directional decisions. In many cases, driving requires the driver to make decisions based on the affect that choices may have on others. For example, a driverless car cannot make the decision to run a red light because it is to dangerous to brake on icy roads. A driverless car cannot asses the situation, consider the effects on others in an emotional sense, and make a decision. A driverless car can only do what it is programmed to do; it cannot think. Driverless cars are dangerous due to the fact that technolgy is not designed to weigh emotional situations.

In many states, testing driverless cars is illegal. It is also illegal to operate a driverless car in many states. This is due to the fact driving laws were designed for cars operated by actual people. "Presently, traffic laws are written with the assumption that the only safe car has a human driver in control at all times". (P.9) The safety of driverless cars is such a controversy because of legal reasons. Machines were designed to be "perfect"; they were designed to factor out human error. Even though machines eliminate human error, machines can fail. The safety of driverless cars is a concern because the opperator is constanly at risk of technological failure The driverless car could fail, resulting in an accident or death. Legally, "if the technology fails and somebody is injured, who is at fault- the driver or the manufacturer?" Driverless cars are such a legal controvery to due risks of injury caused by technological failure.

In conclusion, technology is an ever-advancing, time saving idea that can have negative apsects. Technology takes away from human/mechanical interaction. Specifically, driverless cars are dangerous and they take away necessary human control. They are a risk and a danger to those operating driverless cars. Driverless cars can also be dangerous to those around the operator.

essay_text:
In the past ten years, humans have experienced an unprecedented growth in technology. This time is not called "The Age of the Internet" for no reason, and it is clear that technology is still rapidly advancing. But are all of these developments truly beneficial? As humanity progresses, new ideas are accpeted out of excitement, ignoring many important concerns such as safety. Self-driving cars are a new development that many are interested in, but are considered widely controversial because of the dangers they have. Although self-driving cars seem effective on paper, their technological limitations along with the need for constant human involvement makes them cumbersome, dangerous, and ineffective.

Due to driverless cars being an unprecedented invention, many news outlets have included details on their development. Most of these also include details on its growth and future, and also its flaws. Driverless cars, while theoretically solving many issues with transportation, are too significantly limited by technology to be practical. According to the article "Driverless Cars Are Coming," a driverless car needs lots of expensive technology to function, such as the LIDAR, a spinning sensor mounted on the roof of the car, and position-estimating sensors on the left rear wheel. These are the cars that rely on human involvement to be more or less driverless, so having a truly self-driving car would require even more equipment. With more technology, the vehicle's price skyrockets, making it much more expensive compared to other cars. Additionally, maintence and repair would also be drastically increased to accomdate these many changes. With these factors in mind, it is difficult to see how self-driving cars can gain a sizable portion of the market, and thus unable to be a sustainable industry.

Another factor that disavows the benefits of self-driving cars are their limitations. According to "Driverless Cars Are Coming," self-driving cars need the driver to be alert and ready to respond to complex driving situations. Not only does this defeat the purpose of a self-driving car, it also shows one of the severe limations that this car has. The driver has to be constantly aware of the situation to make these decisions, which means that the car can only move in basic functions. Although this is not a problem in a place where the car can respond effectively, such as a suburb or city, in rural areas or other places with varying terrain, it will be completely ineffective to have a self-driving car. Many different companies would try to create a car that can respond to these systems well. However, this promotes danger and can result in more accidents than if the driver was simply operating the car themselves.

Amongst these other problems, the topic of safety is one of the most influential. in the article "Driverless Cars Are Coming," only a few states offer limited use of semi-automated cars, and the vast majority outright ban it. It is evident that most of these states focus on the safety aspects of the car when discussing its legality. With these problems, the situation of failing technology can and will occur, which can result in human injury. It is clear that many of the possible situations in which

the car had an accident could be remedied by human drivers. "Driverless Cars Are Coming" mentions the situation where a driverless car has an accident, and who is to blame, the driver or the manufacturer? Such conflicts between the two groups would arise of these situation becomes a reality. Conflicts and changes among traffic laws, taxi drivers, and other businesses that rely on transportation would be significantly impacted if driverless cars come in to play.

All in all, the limitations that concern driverless cars are too major to ignore, and will likely be the reason that self-driving cars will not be a feasible reality. While some of these problems can be remedied over time, the current situation of driverless cars is that of a dream.

essay_text:
The rapid increase in technology has created seemingly limitless possibilities for the future; however, this rise is accompanied humans pressing toward the boundaries of numerous ethicial and moral standards. One such ethical line that could be crossed in the near future is the privacy of an individual. We are reaching a society where in time, it would be feasible to monitor every single living human on earth at the exact same time. With this in mind, people are asking themselves the question of where would the boundary be between the invasion of an individual's privacy and the collective good a worldwide security system could be. In his article, "Making Mona Lisa Smile," Nick D'Alto outlines new software that could, if further enhanced, allow students' facial expressions to be read during classrooms. While Prof. Thomas Huang believes that this could possibly increase the quality of learning in the classroom, I believe that it is too much of a privacy breach that to be allowed inside an classroom due to its ability to store faces, constant observance of people, and implications if allowed inside a classroom.

The ability of the Facial Action Coding System is that it is able to scan a person's face and give the emotion of the person. At first glance, this invention may seem to be great inside the classroom because as Dr. Huang said, "A classroom computer could recongize when a student is becoming confused or bored... Then it could modify the lesson, like an effective human instructor" (6). When digging deeper, however, there are a lot of risks and ethical boundaries that are being crossed if this technology were further developed.

In order for the Facial Action Coding System to be able to determine a person's emotion, according to paragraph 3, it must first scan a person's face and render a 3D model of the face wtih all the facial muscles in the face. Then the machine can use this model to determine through muscle movement the emotional expression of the person. This sounds amazing; however, there are many security implication with this technology, specifically the need to scan a person's face. This software needs to construct a very realistic 3-D model of the face, and it has to use this 3-D to determine a person's muscle position. Therefore, this software would need to save this person's facial expression and then analyze, but there are some security risks with having a machine save and analyze students' facial expressions. If a security breach were to happen in the school, every single human in the school would now have their face accessible by the infiltrator. This is a very serious risk to many people who wish to not have arguably the most personal information stolen from them. The face is seemingly the last defense against identity theft and crimes of those sorts. With facial recognition software being stolen, many people would be facing serious risk of being hacked or other crimes. Even if the computer did not store the face, an infiltrator could bug the computer and have every person's face when being analyzed also feed into their private computer. The possibilities for security breaches are just too much of a risk to allow this technology inside a classroom.

Some supporters of this technology might argue that humans also analyze emotions and it is not different if a machine does it as the machine just does it better. Unfortunately, this is not the case. Like what Dr. Huang said, "humans perform this same 'impressive' calculation every day. For instance, you can probably tell how a friend is feeling simply by the look on their face " (5). But Dr. Huang does not realize that humans do not create a 3-D model of each person's face. Humans do not keep copies of every person's face that they have met. As humans, people forget stuff easily, but that is not the same with computer who can accurately keep an exact copy of everything they see.

If the Facial Action Coding System were implimented inside a classroom, it would also be in constant obserbance of everyone. This would be a major ethical breach as it would almost universally be considerd invasion of privacy. Unlike other old technology such as security cameras, this machine would have to be constantly analyzing every person's emotion in order to determine whether students understand or not. This is because as Dr. Huang said, "A classroom computer could recongize when a student is becoming confused or bored... Then it could modify the lesson, like an effective human instructor" (6). However, for this to be able to work well, the computer would need inputs from the students all the time. For many, imagining their faces being analyzed constantly during school would not be fun. It would put immense pressure on the students to pay attention and do well in the class as they are constantly being observed. This pressure and all-knowing eye (the computer) would in the long term, be detrimental to the students' performance as many will be burned out by the immense scrutiny the machine places on them to look like their paying attention and to look not confused. And the human face is much more complex than numbers. People are not just "83 percent happy, 9 percent disgusted, 6 percent fearful, and 2 percent angry" (1). Computers can not understand humans. They can only objectify and analyze through numbers. Unlike what Dr. Huang said, "So computers need to understand [nonverbal comuncation, too" (6), computers can not understand faces. They only can crunch numbers and give percentages.

In conclusion, the Facial Action Coding System should not be implemented inside a classroom. This is because it is a risk to people's security due to its storing of people's faces and how it can observe everyone at the same time in an act of privacy invasion. While the invention is a marvelous thing, they are numerous changes that need to be made in order for it to be classroom friendly and safe. Machines are the future of the new world. They have processing speed but lack the ability to understand.

In the article "The Challenge of Exploring Venus," the author is suggesting that, despite its dangers, the planet Venus is worth putting time and resources towards learning more about the planet itself.

The auothor brings up many points on why it is so difficult to explore the planet. As the author stated in paragraph two, "no spacecraft survived the landing for more than a few hours". This can already be discouraging to some people, but others might think something is wrong with the technology we put there. In paragraph three we really start to see why the planet is so dangerous, "A thick atmosphere of almost 97 percent carbon dioxide blankets Venus. Even more challenging are the clouds of highly corrosive sulfuric acid in Venus's atmosphere. On the planet's surface, temperatures average over 800 degrees Fahrenheit, and the atmospheric pressure is 90 times greater than what we eperience on our own planet. [...] Venusian geology and weather present additional impediments like erupting volcanoes, powerful earthquakes, and frequent lightning strikes to probes seeking to land on its surface". At this point it sounds impossible to even be able to study Venus. Really, why would we want to study Venus? In paragraph four the author explains this, "Astronomers are fascinated by Venus because it may well once have been the most Earth-like planet in our solar system. [...] The planet has a surface of rocky sediment and includes familiar features such as valleys, mountains, and craters".

So we know that the surface of the planet has some of the harshest conditions and that the atmosphere is filled with acid, but the planet is similar to earth in structure. How would we be able to get to it and study it? In paragraph five the author begins to explain some ideas NASA has for being able to study Venus, "NASA's possible solution to the hostile conditions on the surface of Venus would allow scientists to float above the fray. Imagine a blimp-like vehicle hovering 30 or so miles above the roiling Venusian landscape. [...] At thirty-plus miles above the surface, temperatures would still be toasty at around 170 degrees Fahrenheit, but the air pressure would be close to that of sea level on Earth. Solar power would be plentiful, and radiation would not exceed Earth levels. Not easy conditions, but survivable for humans". The important thing about this quote is that humans could survive in this blimp-like vehicle. However, if this were to happen scientists still would not be able to collect samples from Venus, they would just be able to observe it. The author also explains some electronics and machines NASA is looking into in paragraph seven, "some simplified electronics made of silicon carbide have been tested in a chamber simulating the chaos of Venus's surface and have lasted for three weeks in such conditions. [...] mechanical computers [...] these devices make calculations by using gears and levers and do not require electronics at all." These two ideas could be very useful for learning about Venus. The first one made of silicon carbide has already been able to survive for three weeks in similar conditions to Venus, and could be very important for landing on Venus. The author also brought up mechanical computers. The computers don't require electronics, as they said, and can much less fragile than modern computers. These could allow for some intersting ways to learn about Venus.

The author explained the dangers of Venus and why no mission has been very succesful. They also explained ideas for future missions that could possibly involves sending humans. I like the ideas they have, but I do not think the solutions are quite ready for humans to go to Venus. I am very excited to see where this goes and what new ideas scientists come up with.

Limiting car use would be an improvement to society on a global scale. According to source 1, passenger cars are responsible for 12 percent of greenhouse gas emissions in Europe. Furthermore, there is more justification for the advantages of limiting car usage. According the source 2, congestion was down 60 percent in the capital of France. This was due to the fact that Paris enforced a partial driving ban to clear the air five days before. The amount of time it took to air out smog that rivaled Beijing,China is an impressive factor of being fuel efficient. However, some would argue that limiting vehicle use is not the route to go. According to source 2, when the Paris driving ban was enforced, delivery companies complained of lost profit. essay_text:
Technology has always been evolving. From the first consumer computers in the 1980's, to the mainframe era of the past decades, to the complicated and complex computers we have today, technology has always been getting more advanced. Yet, at each rung in the ladder of technology, there has always been new technologies claiming to be the "next greatest thing," but falling short of just that.

In this latest step of technology evolution, people have began to develop AI--artificial intelligence--in order to learn like humans, be like humans, and recognize patterns like humans. The Facial Action Coding System is one of these latest AIs, developed by Professor Thomass Huang of the Beckman Institute for Advanced Science in the University of Illinois, and claiming to be the next greatest thing in the classroom, being able to read student's emotional expressions in the learning environment and react accordingly. Yet, Huang's technology is a double-edged sword. The use of facial emotion recognization in the classroom is a misguided attempt at innovation in technology, and will only lead to an invasion of privacy and increased expenses due to processing power requirements; additionally, people who argue that this technology will help students learn clearly do not recognize the problems in conflict with traditional classrooms and effectiveness.

The use of facial emotion recognization in the classroom will not be a valuable tool, and instead will lead to an invasion of privacy and a conflict with laws. Facial recognition would require sensors everywhere, namely cameras constantly focused on the student. As Huang says himself, emotional "communication is nonverbal [...] so computers need to understand that" (Paragraph 6). In order to improve software products, user input is almost always required. Much of the software used today follows this type of mindset. When programs crash, they send data of the user and what they are doing to the product creator. Thus, a facial recognization program, in order to improve its accuracy and "understand [emotions]", would need to send data about the user to the programmer. At its best, this would be simple information about what is on the computer. At its worst, this would lead to a big-brother like state in schools, with pictures being sent constantly. However, most likely, the product will send pictures whenever the programmer wants to improve their product. Whichever of these situations occurs, a violation of privacy is automatically in place, even if it is just sending small amounts of information about the computer, as not all students would consent. Laws already exist to prevent all of these collections of data on children, laws such as COPA, which would prove a massive problem to facial recognization in attempts to improve software, enough of a problem to completly kill off facial recognization in the classroom.

The use of facial emotion recognization in the classroom is not a valuable tool, and will fail due to the lack of technologic powers and increased cost. Although emotion recognization seems to be a great, bleeding-edge tool which will help with the future, one of its fundamental flaws is the technology required. As mentioned by the author, "your home PC can't handle the complex algorithms used" in the facial recognization process (Paragraph 7). Therefore, how can schools, many without proper funding for technology, be expected to meet the technologic requirements for running facial recognization? They can't. Even with the previous trend of exponential growth in the complexity of technology, schools will not be able to meet the needs to facial recognization. Modern-day computers that run the technology are very advanced, costing thousands of dollars. With the rising prices of technology and the decreasing rate of growth in the complexity of technology, facial recogization will become something that only expensive computers can run. The truth is, many schools run on very outdated technology and cannot afford to buy new expensive computers simply for one task. One might suggest that governments completly cover the cost of technology for public and private schools, but this would provide a massive burden on the US, having to provide hundreds of thousands of computers, and expensive ones too. This cost would be better spend on providing education for people in need, rather than more novelties and niceties for priviledged students. Additionally, only a few large companies supply learning products to schools. With this type of facial recognization, there would be little competition due to the complex nature required to code and run the product. With less competition, monopolization of a product becomes easier, which would drive costs up more.

Although some people may argue that the use of facial emotion recognization in the learning environment is valuable and could help boost student's learning, they clearly do not see the potential for abuse and conflict with classroom types; nor do they see the key problem with facial recognization in the classroom: the simple fact that it has not been proven effective. Modern day classrooms are simple: the teacher teaches, the student sits and learns. This method has not changed for decades, despite the advancement of technology. New tech may provide some form of online learning, but most students still partipate in a traditional classroom. Due to this, the use of tech may help students learn, but tech is typically not the only things students learn from. Teachers still exist to help. However, with facial recognization, schools would require students to be constantly on technology, with software constantly gauging their learning rates, "recogniz[ing] when a student is becoming confused or bored" (Paragraph 6). This would invalidate the traditional classroom setting, making teachers obsolete. A shift in how classrooms are operated would be required, a shift that many people are reluctant to do. Additionally, some students would not benefit as well from learning on computers. Some students learn verbally. Some graphically. Some with their hands. Some students simply cannot learn with technology, or have hard time doing so. Using facial recognization would provide a disvantage to these students. However, all of this ignores the one most major problem of facial recognization in the learning environment: the fact that it has not been scientifically proven. Studies have been run on how facial cues impact emotion, such as the "Facial Feedback Theory of Emotion" which suggest that "moving your facial muscles not only expresses emotions, but also may help produce them" (Paragraph 9). Yet, no studies have been run on how facial recognizing would benefit, or not benefit, students in the classroom.

Facial emotion recognization in the classroom is not a valuable technology for use for students in the classroom due to privacy invasions, cost, and not being scientifically studied. In order to improve the software, privacy would have to be invaded in order to improve accuracy rates and prevent software errors. The cost of such technology would be massive, requiring computers that have massive amounts of processing power, a cost that schools and governments cannot afford. This cost can be put to other things than facial recognization in the classroom. Likewise, facial recognization has not even been scientifically tested to work in the classroom. People who suggest that facial recognization could help in the classroom do not realize these fallacies preventing it from working. Even though technology has been evolving, facial recognization in the classroom is simply another technology that will not work and will die out, as have many other technologies that people have claimed to be the next greatest thing.

essay_text:
In a world as driven as ours, technology is constantly exploding with new advancements. The ideas that we thought of as fantasy are now becoming reality. However, not all advancements are good advancements, as a new idea does not always equal a good idea. This may be the case with the Facial Action Coding System, also known as the FACS, which is an item that helps recognize emotions by scanning a human's facial expression. This technology is suggested for advertisers or lie detector tests, but one slightly different angle on the item is to use the facial scan in classrooms to assess how students are feeling during a lesson. However, his could lead to many miscommunications and misunderstandings. The negative aspects of this product being used in classrooms far outweigh the postives, which is why the Facial Action Coding System is not a valuable tool to read the emotional expressions of students in a classroom.

One large issue of the Facial Action Coding System being used in classroom is that fact that not every student will want their emotions to be known. In some cases, it could become an issue of student privacy. In the article "Making Mona Lisa Smile," it is proposed, "Imagine being able to detect exactly how other people are feeling, even when they are trying to hide their emotions." While this idea is very intruiging, it could be an invasion of an individual's privacy. If someone does not want their true thoughts and emotions to be known, then they should not be exposed. Classrooms are supposed to allow students to feel safe, and should give all pupils an outlet to express themselves. If the Facial Action Coding System is implemented into classrooms, it could destroy the quality of a school environment. Because it could be an invasion of privacy, the Facial Action Coding System would not be valuable when used in a classroom.

Not only could the Facial Action Coding System impede on students' privacy, but it could also have times of failure. In the article, it is said that Dr. Paul Eckman only has six basic emotions that the FACS can recognize. If someone is showing an emotion that is not on that list, it could be read incorrectly. Plus, not every human has the same face structure. While the average person has similar muscle reactions, some people may have a disorder or disfigurement that prohibits the FACS from reading their face correctly. So, because the Facial Action Coding System has too much room for failure, it would not be valuable to use with students in a classroom.

Finally, the Facial Action Coding System would not be valuable in classrooms, because it does not help teach students initiative and agency. In the article, Dr. Huang says, "A classroom computer could recognize when a student is becoming confused or bored. Then it could modify the lesson, like an effective human instructor." If students are constantly exposed to something that adjusts based on their interests, they will never be able to thrive in the real world, as the jobs they obtain in the future will likely not adjust for an individual's preferences. When a student sits through a boring lecture, they must learn how to look past the boredom and build skills to keep obtaining information. If a student becomes confused during a lesson, they must learn how to take initiative, and build their agency skills by asking questions. If the FACS was implemented in schools, students would no longer have to build their agency skills, and would fail in a real world scenario where that ideal is required. Clearly, the Facial Action Coding System would not be a valuable tool in schools, because it would deteriorate students' learning abilities.

Finding new technology advancements is always exciting, but it is clear that not every idea is a good idea. The Facial Action Coding System is a unqiue product, but the ideas that come with this machine can lead to a quality deterioration in student life, especially if the FACS is implemented in schools. The FACS could invade students' privacy, or restrict their agency capabilities. On top of these concerns, the FACS will not always be one hundred percent correct, and there will always be limitations holding it back from being truly useful in a classroom setting. Overall, the negatives of the Facial Action Coding system are much too concerning to be of any value in a school classroom.

essay_text:
Have you ever experienced a whirlwind of emotions during a dull class? It can truly be a difficult experience attempting to explain to your teacher that you need time out from class in order to orient your mind back onto the subject at hand. With a new technology developed called FACS, or Facial Action Coding System, conveying problems to a teacher isn't difficult when that teacher can already read their students' faces. This innovation, created by Prof. Thomas Huang, can detect the emotions of humans through psychology and 3D computer models that track all 44 major muslces in the human a human visage. In a classroom, The use of FACS is undeniably valuable because it can set apart a real smile from a fake smile, understand the basic human emotions need to be identified in a classroom setting, and it could provide an easier learning environment for students with social issues.

Setting apart a real smile versus a fake smile can provide value in the way a teacher interacts with their student. These feelings are understood with the help of psychology from Dr Paul Eckman. The FACS aparatus uses 6 basic human emotions that can inevitably seperate a real smile versus a fake smile. As it states in the article, "These muscle clues are sometimes used to spot when a "smiling" politician or celebrity isn't being truthful." Therefore, in a classroom setting, differintiating real vs fake can be valuable when a student isn't being truthful about their dog eating their homework. It can also decrease the amount of anxiety a student recieves when asked if they understand a subject, in front of the class. If a teacher can understand their emotions, it would facilitate having to step aside with the student and talk about their difficulties on a particular subject. Understanding the difference between a faux emotion and a real one can make a difference in the realm of teaching, just as understanding the six basic emotions can too.

Using psychology, the teacher can also understand the six basic emotions needed to understand in a classroom setting. With the help of psychologist Dr Paul Eckman, the emotions read by FACS are happiness, surprise, anger, disgust, fear, and sadness. Happiness and surprise are emotions most of us can read, therefore its understanding through the FACS machine isn't vital to be understood. Other emotions like anger can be important though. Identifying anger on the face of a student can keep other students safe by isolating the student from the others, which in turn could keep everyone safe from a potential rage-induced tantrum. It can also help knowing berforehand if a student is sensing dangerous emotions such as anger, fear and/or sadness. According to paragraph 9, "Empathy may happen because we unconsciously imitate another person's facial expressions." If a teacher knows some of her students are feeling any of the three previous emotions, producing a happy environment could help keep the students happy or bring their emotions to a higher level of happiness. It therefore is valuable in that aspect and also to students thank can have issues with talking to their teacher.

Just as identifying dangerous emotions is important, so is identifying uncomfortable situations that can therefore be easier for socially anxious students. According to Dr. Huang, "A classroom computer could recognize when a student is becoming confused or bored." The former of those two emotions is vital to be identified because it can help teacher know when their students don't truly understand a lesson. Often, socially anxious students tend to keep their quesitons to themselves when they are confused about a topic out of fear of being juged as dumb or incompetent. They will go on without understanding which in turn can cause a decrease in their grades. If a teacher is able to read the true expression of their student, these type of situations can be avoided in order to create a better teaching experience for all the students.

In conclusion, FACS is without a doubt a valuable asset in a classroom environment because of its ability to identify real from fake, its understanding of the basic human emotions needed to be comprehended in a class, and because it facilitates learning for students with a fear of speaking.

In the future, seeing this innovation in a class setting would be an amazing feat for a step towards smarter teaching. With the help of Prof. Thomas Huang and Dr. Paul Eckman, a new future can be created.

essay_text:
The development of driverless cars is certainly an indicator that technology has come a long way since Henry Ford and the Model-T. However, it is disappointing that the technology at hand is being used to encourage sloth, laziness, and lackadaisical behavior.

Above all, the whole idea of driverless cars is a dangerous one. If drivers aren't alert and ready at the wheel in a precarious position, which there is bound to be, then it would result in an automotive accident that could cost someone his life, or a major lawsuit against the car company that didn't need to happen. The time spent developing these automated creations could be better spent creating other automated products.

Overall, humanity has to be productive. Productivity solves problems; Laziness solves nothing. In a time when there are many problems to be solved: global warming, cancer, religious extremism, civil rights, humans don't have time to waste. The projected ideas presented within "Driverless Cars Are Coming" lead the reader to assume that the only way drivers wouldn't need to drive at all, is if every single car owner owned one of these smart cars. Then humans wouldn't need to drive, the systems used in the cars would probably show the best routes and make better choices, as well as prevent obstacles like traffic jams, but even if it worked big cities like New York and Chicago, where high traffic is incessant, this still wouldn't be practical. This leaves the human operator

the car wasting time, rather than doing something productive or worthwhile.

The whole idea of smart cars is dangerous. Some may deem it impractical, but seeing how far technology has progressed already, it isn't a large jump from driverless cars to driverless cars with wifi, and from there not much of a jump from car jackers to car hackers. The inevitable connection of these cars to form a more optimal system of driving as far as traffic is concerned poses a problem: system breaches. If an unknown entity were to hack one of the computer operated driverless cars and decide to tamper with sensors or other facilities, that could put a human in peril. If a driver isn't paying attention when the car signals to take over, or can't react fast enough, that also could result in danger for the operator.

There are much better uses for the systems and devices involved in driverless cars. The sensors could create amazing security systems. The GPS has a massive variety of uses already being employed today. The technology could create drones to assist in jobs humans can't do already. There's a vast amount of untapped potential that could be dangerously wasted on cars.

The driverless car creates opportunities. Opportunities to put others in danger, yet opportunities to give people a break. However, the driverless car must be taken with a grain of salt. Carefully examined, thoroughly protected, an eye catching piece of metal for the driver. Driverless cars have to come a long way before being ready for the public.
